n.description: Number of vertices in the perfect graph

m.description: Number of edges in the perfect graph

E.description: Edges of the graph; each row lists the two endpoint vertices of an edge, with the smaller vertex index listed first

P.description: Number of disjoint clusters partitioning the vertex set

clusterSize.description: Number of vertices in each cluster

clusters.description: Vertex indices belonging to each cluster

q.description: Number of maximal cliques of the graph

cliqueSize.description: Number of vertices in each maximal clique

K.description: Vertex indices belonging to each maximal clique of the graph

assumptions.description: The number of vertices is positive.
The number of clusters is positive.
Each cluster contains at least one vertex.

code.python: assert all(clusterSize[p] >= 1 for p in range(P))

assumptions.description: The clusters partition the vertex set: every vertex belongs to exactly one cluster.

code.python: assert sum(clusterSize) == n and sorted(i for cluster in clusters for i in cluster) == list(range(n))

assumptions.description: The number of maximal cliques is positive.
Each maximal clique contains at least one vertex with a valid vertex index.

code.python: assert all(cliqueSize[l] >= 1 and all(0 <= i <= n - 1 for i in K[l]) for l in range(q))

assumptions.description: Every edge connects two distinct, valid vertex indices.

code.python: assert all(0 <= i < j <= n - 1 for i, j in E)

assumptions.description: Each K_l lists distinct vertices and forms a clique of the graph: every pair of distinct vertices in K_l is connected by an edge.
assumptions.formulation: K_{l,a} \neq K_{l,b} \ \wedge\ \{K_{l,a}, K_{l,b}\} \in E \quad \forall l \in \{0,\ldots,q-1\}, \ a \neq b

assumptions.description: K covers every clique of the graph: since K enumerates all maximal cliques, every clique (maximal or not) is a subset of the vertex set of some K_l.
assumptions.formulation: \forall S \subseteq \{0,\ldots,n-1\} \text{ a clique}, \ \exists l \in \{0,\ldots,q-1\}: S \subseteq K_l

x.description: Equals 1 if vertex i is selected, 0 otherwise

t.description: Estimate of the number of colors needed to color the selected vertices

constraints.description: Exactly one vertex is selected from each cluster.

code.gurobipy:
for p in range(P):
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(x[i] for i in clusters[p]) == 1)

constraints.description: t is at least the number of selected vertices within any maximal clique of the graph; since the graph is perfect, the largest such count over all maximal cliques equals the chromatic number of the induced subgraph on the selected vertices.

code.gurobipy:
for l in range(q):
    model.addConstr(t >= gp.quicksum(x[i] for i in K[l]))

objective.description: Minimize the number of colors needed to color the selected vertices.

notes: Reformulates Model 2 of Seker, Ekim, and Taskin (2021) as a static MILP.

The paper's Model 2 bounds t below by chi(G[x]), the chromatic number of the subgraph induced by the selection x; this is not a linear expression, so the paper resolves it with cutting planes t >= sum_{i in K^(j)} x_i generated iteratively from a maximum clique K^(j) of G[x^(j)] found at each step of a cutting-plane algorithm, rather than a single static formulation.

Since the input graph is perfect, chi(G[x]) = omega(G[x]) (clique number) for every selection x, and omega(G[x]) equals the size of the largest maximal clique of the full graph G that lies entirely within the selection. We therefore replace the paper's dynamically generated cuts with the full, static set of constraints t >= sum_{i in K_l} x_i for every maximal clique K_l of G, precomputed once from the input graph in gen_params.py.

q, cliqueSize, and K are derived parameters computed from the base graph parameters (n, m, E) via maximal-clique enumeration; m and E are carried through from problem.json unchanged, while q, cliqueSize, and K are specific to this formulation and not part of problem.json.
